Steal My 15-Minute Design Rule

I Put a Timer on My Creativity. Here’s What Happened.

Saloni Malhotra
3 min read
·
Just now

--

I wish I had learned this design rule much earlier. A few years ago, when I was constantly overthinking my designs and feeling frustrated every time I opened Figma, one of my seniors shared a simple trick he had been following for years. He told me something that stuck with me: every designer feels overwhelmed at some point. It doesn’t matter if you’re a beginner designing your first landing page or a senior designer with years of experience. Everyone reaches a stage where they feel stuck, frustrated, and unsure of their decisions. The difference is that experienced designers don’t rely on motivation or inspiration every day — they build systems that help them keep moving. His system was surprisingly simple:

Design with a Timer

Since then, I’ve followed the same rule, and honestly, I never design without a timer. The moment I open Figma, I start a 15-minute timer. No expectations. No pressure to create something amazing. No goal of designing an entire website in one sitting. Just one section and fifteen minutes. If I’m working on a landing page, I give myself 15 minutes for the hero section. Once that’s done, I set another 15 minutes for the next section. Then another 15 minutes for the page structure. The goal isn’t perfection — it’s progress. Instead of trying to solve everything at once, I focus on building one piece at a time.

The reason this works is surprisingly simple.

Unlimited time creates overthinking.

When there’s no deadline, it’s easy to overthink everything. You keep changing fonts, trying different colors, moving things around, and wondering if there’s a better way to design it. Instead of actually building the page, you spend most of your time thinking about what to do next. Before you know it, hours have passed and the design is still incomplete.

A timer fixes that. When you only have 15 minutes, you stop chasing perfection and start making decisions. You trust your instincts, choose a direction, and keep moving. You don’t waste time redesigning the same thing again and again. The timer creates focus, helps you work faster, and keeps you from getting stuck in endless overthinking.

What most designers do is the exact opposite. They spend thirty minutes redesigning one button. Then they redesign it again. Then they compare it with three other versions. Then they change the font. Then they change the spacing. Then they start doubting the entire layout. After a while, they feel overwhelmed, frustrated, and mentally exhausted. Eventually, many of them close Figma for the day feeling like they’ve worked hard but accomplished very little.

The funny thing is that creativity doesn’t thrive when there are endless possibilities. Creativity loves constraints. It loves deadlines. It loves pressure.

Some of the best ideas appear when there isn’t enough time to overthink them.When you only have fifteen minutes, your brain focuses on what matters most instead of getting lost in tiny details. You stop asking, “What’s the perfect design?” and start asking, “What’s the best design I can create right now?”

That’s why the 15-minute rule has become a permanent part of my workflow. It helps me design faster, make better decisions, and enjoy the process far more than I used to. More importantly, it reminds me that great design isn’t about endlessly refining the same button or searching for perfection. It’s about building momentum, trusting your instincts, and continuing to move forward. Sometimes, all it takes is a simple timer ticking in the corner of your screen.
